title: Pattern Candidates To Add
description: Pattern candidates surfaced during composition research but not yet catalogued. Each entry names what the pattern would forbid or enable, the composition that raised it, and the upstream evidence. Entries graduate by being authored into patterns-src/ and removed from this list.

candidates.name: Agent Framework Bloat
candidates.summary: Stacking heavy abstractions above the model call — chains, runnables, callbacks, agents, executors — that obscure the underlying tool-call loop. smolagents argues for staying ~1k LoC and minimal abstractions above raw code.

raised_by.note: Stated design value in smolagents docs: 'simplicity' as an explicit anti-bloat stance.

evidence.title: smolagents index — Simplicity
evidence.quote: Simplicity: The logic for agents fits in ~thousand lines of code. We kept abstractions to their minimal shape above raw code!

candidates.rejected_reason: Framework design stance rather than a recurring problem with cross-evidence. The complaint surfaces only as one framework's marketing differentiator (smolagents); no second independent named occurrence of the same problem with a competing fix. Catalog's bar (recurring problem across products/years) is not met. Reconsider if 2+ independent sources name this anti-pattern with the same shape.

candidates.name: Speaker Selection by Model
candidates.summary: An LLM picks the next-speaker in a multi-agent conversation based on shared context — a narrower-than-supervisor pattern where the model only routes, not plans.

raised_by.note: SelectorGroupChat uses an LLM for next-speaker selection.

evidence.title: AutoGen — SelectorGroupChat
evidence.quote: A team that selects the next speaker using a ChatCompletion model after each message

candidates.rejected_reason: Subsumed as the 'Selector (LLM-Chosen)' variant of group-chat-manager (authored 2026-05-20). GoF Variants slot is the right home: same problem, same forces, different selection tactic. Promoting to a first-class pattern would split a single intent across two entries.

candidates.name: Pluggable Reasoning Strategy
candidates.summary: Agent's reasoning shape (e.g. ReAct vs Function Calling) is selected via a plugin contract rather than baked in; consumers can install community-authored strategies as marketplace artefacts.

raised_by.note: Dify exposes Agent Strategy as a plugin contract.

evidence.title: Dify docs — Agent Strategy plugin
evidence.quote: An Agent Strategy Plugin helps an LLM carry out tasks like reasoning or decision-making, including choosing and calling tools, as well as handling results.

candidates.rejected_reason: Single-vendor evidence (Dify only) and the cross-framework analogue is already covered by tool-agent-registry / agent-skills (which package reasoning shapes as installable artefacts). No independent second source names a 'pluggable reasoning strategy' contract with the same intent. Reconsider if a peer framework adopts the same explicit contract and the shape differs meaningfully from existing patterns.

candidates.name: Triangulated Synthesis
candidates.summary: Three-role epistemic check on load-bearing claims: L1 answers in trusting mode, L2 answers in critical mode, L3 acts as a Solomon judge that reconciles. Distinct from debate (two-sided) and self-consistency (vote across samples) — three roles with an explicit judge and a permanent markdown artefact written into memory.

raised_by.note: Sparrot invokes triangulation only on questions she judges epistemically load-bearing (~30–40s, high-cost). The runner executes L1 trusting → L2 critical → L3 Solomon judge in series and writes a permanent .md report into memory.

candidates.rejected_reason: Subsumed as a 3-role variant of inner-committee (single agent, several voices, one output) and debate (multi-position structured exchange). L1-trusting / L2-critical / L3-Solomon-judge is a specific protocol within that family rather than a distinct intent; the permanent-artefact requirement belongs to decision-log / provenance-ledger composition. Promoting would split a single intent across two entries (GoF: intent is what distinguishes patterns of identical structure).

candidates.name: Named Meditation Technique
candidates.summary: Named mind-hygiene techniques (pranayama, samatha, vipassana, etc.) the agent can invoke as discrete moves, distinct from a runtime meditation mode that pauses outward I/O. Each technique is a labelled internal action with its own protocol, callable explicitly.

raised_by.note: kitest carries two distinct modules: meditation_mode.py (runtime mode where I/O is paused) and meditation.py (library of named techniques). The catalog covers the mode but not the techniques.

candidates.rejected_reason: Subsumed as content rather than shape. meditation-mode already captures the structural pattern (bounded runtime mode where I/O pauses and inner tools take over). Pranayama, samatha, vipassana etc. are *which* internal moves run inside that mode — a library of named techniques is a deployment choice for the mode, not a separate agentic-design pattern with its own forces. No second independent source names 'named meditation technique' as a recurring agent design move.

candidates.name: Peer Mailbox
candidates.summary: Agents communicate by writing per-message .md files into peer-specific inbox/replies folders on a shared filesystem. Pure file IO, no network or LLM in the transport, peer ids picked by senders. Distinct from MCP (RPC over a server) and inter-agent-communication (in-process messages).

raised_by.note: Sparrot's peer_messaging module exposes peer_send / peer_replies / list_peers over queue/peers/<peer>/inbox and queue/peers/<peer>/replies folders, one .md per message; atelier is one such peer among others.

candidates.rejected_reason: Subsumed as a file-system-based variant of actor-model-agents. The pattern's essential shape (per-actor mailbox, async messages, no shared mutable state) is already captured; '.md files in a shared filesystem' is a transport choice, not a new intent. Belongs in actor-model-agents.variants[] as a tactical variant if ever needed, not as a first-class pattern.
